facade_colour: black-painted storefront beneath dark red-brown brick upper facade

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as an early-twentieth-century, two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure which is not visible from the public realm. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s pattern of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed between 1914 and 1930, the additions have retained building features reflecting early-twentieth-century vernacular architectural styles, including their brick facades and second storey window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. Contextually, each property is important in maintaining and supporting the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way NB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Primary commercial spine | Pedestrian Sunday route

assessment.signage: For Lease / Philip Park signs visible on boarded-up shutters

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: black-painted ground floor storefront with dense graffiti murals; dark red-brown brick above with some graffiti
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Ground floor is heavily altered with elaborate skeleton/Day-of-Dead mural on roll-down shutter (skeleton playing guitar, flames), graffiti art-covered door on left side, adjacent dark glass door on right with graffiti; upper brickwork remains intact but weathered.
photo_observations.window_details: Large mural-covered storefront opening at grade and three narrow upper-storey windows with dark surrounds visible above the sign band.
photo_observations.door_details: Single commercial entry integrated into the dark-painted storefront surround.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Shallow projecting soffit and metal fascia across the storefront line.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney visible from this frontage view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Brick voussoirs visible over upper-floor window openings despite graffiti. Cornice line at rooftop.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Flat roof concealed by a shallow parapet/soffit line
photo_observations.storefront_description: Dark commercial frontage with elaborate skeleton/Day-of-Dead mural on completely closed roll-down shutter (skeleton playing guitar, flames), graffiti art-covered door on left, dark glass door on right.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Partial side signage for Okay Maguey / adjacent businesses visible at the corner.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Storefront shutters covered in street art and graffiti. Upper brick facade retains segmentally arched window openings as described by HCD.
photo_observations.notes: HCD typology is Commercial (two-storey commercial addition to a hidden residential structure). The visible commercial facade with brick upper storey and segmental arches matches the HCD description. matches_hcd_typology is true because the HCD commercial typology is what we see.

storefront_description: Dark commercial frontage with elaborate skeleton/Day-of-Dead mural on completely closed roll-down shutter (skeleton playing guitar, flames), graffiti art-covered door on left, dark glass door on right.
notes: Photo is focused on the Augusta frontage rather than the side wall.

facade_detail.composition: The street frontage spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys beneath a flat, consistent with its commercial typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The visible opening pattern is organized as 1, 3 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Key storefront and facade features include cornice, decorative brick.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed between 1914 and 1930, the additions have retained building features reflecting early-twentieth-century vernacular architectural styles, including their brick facades and second storey window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. Each property has value as an early-twentieth-century, two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure which is not visible from the public realm.

facade_description: 206 Augusta Ave is a commercial building with a street-level storefront. The street frontage spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys beneath a flat, consistent with its commercial typology. The frontage is defined primarily by mixed in a black-painted storefront beneath dark red-brown brick upper facade palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The visible opening pattern is organized as 1, 3 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Key storefront and facade features include cornice, decorative brick.
heritage_summary: Constructed between 1914 and 1930, the additions have retained building features reflecting early-twentieth-century vernacular architectural styles, including their brick facades and second storey window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. Each property has value as an early-twentieth-century, two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure which is not visible from the public realm.
